intent: Check whether the candidate can identify query explosion from loading related data one row at a time.
primaryQuestion: What is the N+1 query problem? Explain it with a concrete example such as fetching posts and authors.

expectedSignals.label: Defines N+1 as one query for parent rows plus one query per parent for related data.

requirements.description: N parent rows cause 1 + N queries.
Example: fetch posts, then fetch author for each post.
Query count grows linearly with result size.
Network round trips and database work increase even if each query is small.

expectedSignals.label: Gives a concrete query count example.

requirements.description: 100 posts and lazy author loading can produce 101 queries.
A join or batched query can reduce it dramatically.

expectedSignals.label: Mentions it can be hidden by ORM abstractions.

requirements.description: Accessing relation property or serialization can trigger queries silently.
Local small datasets may hide the problem.

scoringHints.description: Covers the mechanism, practical example, edge cases, and production consequence with clear reasoning.
Explains the main idea correctly but misses one nuance such as a boundary case, trade-off, or failure mode.
Shows surface familiarity but cannot apply the concept to a realistic code or API scenario.
Answer is too vague or memorized to validate practical understanding.

intent: Check whether the candidate can choose explicit relation loading without overfetching everything.
primaryQuestion: What are solutions to N+1 in TypeORM, and how do eager loading and QueryBuilder with leftJoinAndSelect differ?

requirements.description: Use relations option, QueryBuilder joins, batched IN queries, projections, or DataLoader depending on context.
Avoids relying on accidental lazy loads.
Eager relation can be automatically loaded by repository find methods.
Too many eager relations can overfetch and slow unrelated queries.
QueryBuilder explicitly joins and selects relation data for one query shape.
It gives control over joins, filters, selected columns, and conditions.
Eager relations are disabled with QueryBuilder in TypeORM, so joins must be explicit.
Candidate knows to use leftJoinAndSelect or explicit selection there.

intent: Assess whether the candidate understands timing of database access and relation loading trade-offs.
primaryQuestion: What is lazy loading versus eager loading in TypeORM, and what are the N+1 risks of lazy loading?

requirements.description: Related data is loaded when relation is accessed.
In TypeORM lazy relations are typically represented by Promise-like relation properties.
Related data is loaded automatically with the parent in certain repository operations.
Eager loading can reduce explicit code for common always-needed relations.
Looping over parents and accessing lazy relation can trigger one query per parent.
Serialization can accidentally load many relations.
Lazy avoids loading unused data but hides query timing.
Explicit query shape is often better for API endpoints.

intent: Check whether the candidate understands batching by IDs and request-scoped caching, especially for GraphQL resolvers.
primaryQuestion: What is DataLoader, how does it solve N+1 through batching, and when is it especially useful?

expectedSignals.label: Defines DataLoader as batching and caching utility.

requirements.description: Loads requested keys in batches instead of one query per field.
Caches results during a request to avoid duplicate loads.
Receives an array of keys or IDs.
Returns values in the same order as keys, including null or errors for missing values as appropriate.
Cache should usually be scoped per request because permissions and data freshness can vary.
Global cache can leak data or become stale.
Each field resolver can request author by ID.
DataLoader batches all author IDs into one IN query for that request.

expectedSignals.label: Explains small local data hides query explosion.

requirements.description: Five rows means six queries may feel fine.

Local database round trips are fast.
Production network, database load, and connection pool contention amplify cost.
Many users causing N+1 endpoints can overload DB connections and CPU.
Slow endpoints increase queueing and timeouts.
Seed realistic data volumes.
Use query logs, APM, and load tests before production.

requirements.description: relations option requests related entities in repository find APIs.

leftJoinAndSelect explicitly controls joins and selected relation data.
QueryBuilder supports more complex filters, conditions, ordering, projections, and joins.
relations can load full entities and more columns than needed.
QueryBuilder can select only required columns or shape a projection.
Use simple relations for simple predictable reads.
Use QueryBuilder for complex filtering, pagination, or performance-sensitive endpoints.

intent: Check whether the candidate balances preventing N+1 with avoiding unnecessary relation loading.
primaryQuestion: What is the risk of marking too many relations as eager: true, and how is overfetching related?

requirements.description: API or query loads more related data or columns than the use case needs.
Extra data increases memory, network, and database work.
Every load of the entity may also load eager relations even for endpoints that do not need them.
Multiple eager relations can create large joins or multiple heavy loads.

expectedSignals.label: Balances N+1 prevention with explicit query shape.

requirements.description: N+1 fix should load exactly the data needed for the endpoint.
Not every relation should be globally eager.
Overfetching can expose fields accidentally if mapping is careless.
Large response payloads can slow clients.

intent: Evaluate whether the candidate can spot N+1 outside obvious repository code.
primaryQuestion: How can lazy loading create hidden database calls during JSON serialization or response mapping?

requirements.description: JSON serialization or DTO mapping reads entity fields.
Reading a lazy relation can trigger a database query.
Mapping a list of entities can trigger one relation query per entity.
Nested mapping can multiply the problem further.
Load required relations explicitly before mapping.
Map from projection/query result rather than rich lazy entities when appropriate.
Do not expose ORM entities directly as API responses.
DTO mapping makes data and relation needs explicit.

intent: Check whether the candidate can apply DataLoader or preloading to field resolver query explosion.
primaryQuestion: How would you solve N+1 in a GraphQL resolver where each Post.author field triggers a database call?

expectedSignals.label: Identifies field resolver source of N+1.

requirements.description: Post list resolver returns many posts.
Author field resolver runs once per post and queries by authorId.

expectedSignals.label: Uses DataLoader batching by author IDs.

requirements.description: Resolver calls authorLoader.load(post.authorId).
Loader batches IDs and performs one query with IN or equivalent.
Each request gets its own loader instance/cache.
Prevents cross-user data leakage and stale global cache.

expectedSignals.label: Handles result ordering and missing rows.

requirements.description: Batch function maps DB results back to requested ID order.
Missing author returns null or appropriate error consistently.

Shows surface familiarity but cannot apply the concept to a realistic code or API scenario.

intent: Evaluate whether the candidate distinguishes parallelizing many queries from reducing query count.
primaryQuestion: Why is Promise.all(items.map(...db query...)) not a real fix for N+1?

expectedSignals.label: Explains Promise.all does not reduce query count.

requirements.description: It still issues one query per item.
It only starts them concurrently.
Parallel queries can overload connection pool or database CPU.
Latency may improve locally while system throughput worsens.
Use joins, batched IN queries, preloading, projection, or DataLoader.
Reduce round trips and query count instead of only parallelizing.
If multiple queries are unavoidable, limit concurrency.
But concurrency limiting is not the same as solving N+1.

intent: Check whether the candidate thinks beyond query count to response shape, memory, and data minimization.
primaryQuestion: How can selecting only required columns help when fixing N+1, and when would you prefer a hand-written projection?

requirements.description: Loading only fields needed by response reduces I/O, memory, and serialization cost.
Avoids loading large text, blobs, or sensitive columns unnecessarily.
Query can return DTO-shaped rows instead of full ORM entities.
Projection can combine related data without lazy relations.
Hand-written SQL or projections can be less reusable than entity loading.
They can be clearer and faster for read-heavy endpoints.

expectedSignals.label: Connects to privacy and API contract.

requirements.description: Response should not expose internal columns accidentally.
Selected fields should match the endpoint contract.

Uses threshold and focused endpoint setup.
